text: The lump spontaneously collapses once air enters the chest.
First, we identify the sympathetic chain which is clearly seen here.
We should note that the first visible rib in Stratoscopy is the second rib because the first rib is not usually seen in the Stratoscopic view.
We usually start by making a hole in the bright blue rack covering the sympathetic chain using an electric dithermy attached to the hook.
Start the incision just lateral to the sympathetic chain over the neck of the third rib.
Then we try to extend the incision to a rib above and a rib below to give us more space for good dissection.
We should note that once the air enters the pleural space, the lung spontaneously collapses to give us enough space, and usually there is no need to keep insufflating gas in high pressure.
Actually, many surgeons claim that they can work without any gas inflation after lung collapse.
Now, with careful blunt dissection, the sympathetic chain is completely visible and accessible.
We can hook it gently to avoid any bleeding or injury to the adjacent vessels.
Once we have a satisfying dissection, we can split the sympathetic chain by the coterie and instead of excising a segment, we can use the coterie to make an efficient cut.
Now we can see that both ends of the sympathetic chain are completely separated and widened.
Many surgeons would bend the pleura extensively over the third rib until they reached the bariosteum of the rib to avoid recurrence by cutting the nerve accounts.
However, a lot of controversy exists around this step because it's thought that this nerve doesn't exist in all people.
So we made a limited burn over this rib here.
Before leaving the chest, we take a final look.
We can see that there is no bleeding at all and the sympathetic chain is completely separated safely.
The lung could be inflated under vision until it completely fills the chest, so there is no need for putting a chest tube at the end of the operation.
